If the plan is tied to milestones, the organisation can tell whether capacity has truly improved rather than merely being discussed. The purpose of the plan is to reduce risk and improve sustainability, not to create another file that no one revisits. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please make sure every capacity-building action has a deadline and evidence of completion.”
Key principle:
- Improvement must be measurable.
- Training is only useful when evidence exists.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- GAO Green Book
Q: How can the organisation use analytics to detect irregular spending patterns in partner files?
A: Show answer
Analytics can be used to compare spending by period, vendor, budget line, staff member, and geography. The team can look for repeated round amounts, concentration of spend near period-end, frequent split invoices, duplicate descriptions, or sudden jumps in one category that are not explained by the workplan. The point is not to accuse the partner automatically. The point is to generate questions that humans can then review with evidence. Even a simple spreadsheet can highlight unusual trends when the same logic is applied consistently across partners. Analytics are most useful when they turn a large file into a short exception list. That lets the team review high-risk items first instead of scanning everything manually. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please review any unusual vendor pattern, round amount, or end-period spike before approving the report.”
Key principle:
- Analytics should surface exceptions, not replace judgment.
- Unusual patterns deserve explanation.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
Q: What should happen if the partner asks to change the payment frequency or advance size?
A: Show answer
The request should be evaluated based on risk, utilisation, reporting quality, and the remaining period of performance. A partner that is spending slowly may not need larger or more frequent advances. A partner that is spending too quickly may need tighter monitoring, not a bigger cash balance. Any change should be backed by cash-flow analysis and approved by the right authority before the new schedule starts. The organisation should not change the payment rhythm casually, because payment frequency is part of the control structure. If the frequency is loosened without reason, the partner can accumulate excess cash. If it is tightened too much, implementation may stall. The right answer is evidence-based, not habitual. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please review any request to change payment frequency or advance size against utilisation and risk.”
Key principle:
- Payment rhythm is a control decision.
- Cash size should match actual need.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.328 Financial reporting
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why should a partner file include a single master timeline of approvals, reports, and payments?
A: Show answer
A master timeline helps the organisation see the full sequence of the award in one place. It should show when the partner was selected, when the assessment was completed, when the agreement was signed, when each payment was made, when each report was due, when monitoring occurred, when findings were cleared, and when close-out happened. Without a timeline, the file becomes a stack of documents without context. With a timeline, reviewers can immediately see whether a payment happened too early, a report was late, or an amendment came after the fact. The timeline is therefore a practical control tool, not just a reference page. It allows finance, programme, audit, and management to read the same history in the same order. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please keep one master timeline for each partner award.”
Key principle:
- Sequence matters as much as content.
- One timeline makes review faster and clearer.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- 2 CFR 200.334 Record retention requirements
Q: How should the organisation deal with repeated late reporting by a partner?
A: Show answer
Repeated late reporting should trigger a structured escalation path. First, the team should confirm whether the delay is caused by missing source documents, weak internal controls, staffing issues, or a genuine operational emergency. Second, the organisation should record the delay history and revise the monitoring plan. Third, it should consider withholding future tranches, reducing the size of advances, or requiring a corrective action plan with deadlines. Late reporting is not only an administrative annoyance; it can hide spending problems and delay the detection of ineligible costs. If the organisation keeps paying on time while reports remain late, it is effectively rewarding weak compliance. The response should therefore be consistent, documented, and proportionate to the pattern. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please escalate repeated late reporting and consider tighter cash control if the pattern continues.”
Key principle:
- Repeated lateness should change the control response.
- Escalation should be documented.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why is a standard reporting template important for partners?
A: Show answer
A standard template makes partner data easier to compare, review, and consolidate. If every partner uses a different format, the finance team spends time remapping columns, chasing missing fields, and correcting inconsistent labels. A standard template should make it clear what periods are covered, what budget lines are being reported, what supporting documents are required, and how exceptions should be explained. It should also include clear instructions on which line items need narrative explanation and which require supporting attachments. The template is a control device because it shapes the quality of the data before it reaches the finance review stage. If the template is good, the review becomes faster and more focused. If the template is poor, even strong finance staff will spend time cleaning basic errors instead of checking real risk. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please use the standard report template for every partner submission.”
Key principle:
- Consistent templates improve control and comparison.
- Reporting quality starts at the input stage.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.328 Financial reporting
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
Q: How should the organisation respond to unsupported or questionable costs in a partner report?
A: Show answer
Questionable costs should not be accepted just because the partner included them in a report. The organisation should ask for the missing support, test whether the cost is allowable and allocable, and confirm whether the charge falls inside the approved scope and period. If the partner cannot provide evidence, the cost may need to be disallowed, reduced, reclassified, or recovered depending on the circumstances. The response should be documented clearly so the partner knows what correction is needed. A structured query log can be helpful here because it records the question, the response, the deadline, and the final disposition. Unsupported costs are a warning sign because they can be symptoms of weak accounting, weak procurement, or weak programme oversight. The organisation should not normalise them. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please query any unsupported cost and do not approve it until the evidence is complete.”
Key principle:
- Unsupported costs need formal resolution.
- Allowability depends on evidence.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.403 Factors affecting allowability of costs
- 2 CFR 200.405 Allocable costs
Q: Why should a partner’s financial report be reconciled to the bank before approval?
A: Show answer
Bank reconciliation proves that the reported cash balance is real and that the transactions in the report can be traced to actual bank movement. If the partner report is approved without reconciliation, the organisation may miss unrecorded withdrawals, duplicate receipts, uncleared payments, or wrong balances. The reconciliation should show the closing bank balance, outstanding items, timing differences, and any unexplained differences. Finance should not approve a report simply because the totals look reasonable. Bank evidence is one of the strongest controls because it comes from outside the partner’s own records. When cash is involved, the report and the bank must tell the same story. If they do not, the issue should be resolved before payment or close-out. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please reconcile the partner’s report to the bank before approval.”
Key principle:
- Bank reconciliation is mandatory before reliance.
- Report and bank evidence must match.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.328 Financial reporting
- GAO Green Book
Q: What should happen when a partner wants to reprogram budget between lines?
A: Show answer
Budget reprogramming should follow the terms of the agreement and the significance of the change. If the movement is minor and within delegated authority, it may be approved at the operational level. If the movement changes the activity emphasis, weakens the original objective, or shifts money into an unapproved category, it should require higher review. The organisation should also consider whether the change affects risk, reporting, or performance monitoring. A reprogramming request should explain the reason, the amount, the original line, the destination line, and the expected effect on outputs. The key is that budget movement must support the award, not simply reflect convenience. A good approval memo creates a permanent record of the decision and its rationale. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please review any budget reprogramming request in line with the agreement and approval limits.”
Key principle:
- Budget movement should have a documented rationale.
- Approval level should match the size and impact of the change.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
Q: How should the organisation treat indirect costs or overhead in a sub-award?
A: Show answer
Indirect costs or overhead should only be charged if the agreement allows them and the basis is clearly stated. The organisation should know whether the rate is fixed, capped, negotiated, or allocated by another method. If the partner charges overhead, finance should verify whether it was budgeted correctly and whether the calculation matches the agreed rate. Overhead should not be used as a catch-all line for unidentified expenses. The treatment should also be consistent across periods so that the partner cannot shift unexplained costs into indirect charges after the fact. Good overhead control protects both the programme and the funder by keeping actual direct costs clean and the shared cost methodology transparent. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please verify the overhead rate and calculation before approving indirect charges.”
Key principle:
- Overhead must be authorised and calculated correctly.
- Indirect charges should not hide unsupported costs.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.405 Allocable costs
- 2 CFR 200.403 Factors affecting allowability of costs
Q: Why is a central sub-award register so useful?
A: Show answer
A central register gives the organisation one place to see every active partner, award number, reporting date, payment status, risk level, amendment history, advance balance, outstanding action, and close-out stage. Without a central register, those facts are usually scattered across email threads, spreadsheets, and staff memory. That makes it hard to answer simple questions such as which partner is overdue, which award is close to expiry, or which report is blocking the next payment. A good register becomes the front page of the entire management process. It helps finance, programme, audit, and management work from the same facts rather than from parallel notes. For a growing portfolio, the register is one of the fastest ways to improve control and visibility at the same time. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please keep one central sub-award register for all active partners.”
Key principle:
- One register improves visibility and control.
- Shared facts reduce duplicated tracking.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
Q: What information should the register show for each partner award?
A: Show answer
The register should show the partner name, award reference, agreement date, start and end dates, budget value, cumulative payments, current advance or settlement balance, risk rating, last report date, next due date, outstanding findings, and close-out status. It should also show whether the agreement has been amended and whether the partner is under a corrective action plan. If possible, the register should separate financial and programme due dates so staff can see whether the delay is on the cash side or the narrative side. A good register is not just a list. It is a control dashboard that supports reminders, escalation, and monthly review. The more complete the field structure, the less staff rely on memory or informal follow-up. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please keep the partner register complete and update it after every report and payment.”
Key principle:
- The register should support action, not just storage.
- Financial and programme dates should be visible together.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
- GAO Green Book
Q: How can reminders and escalation help partner compliance?
A: Show answer
Automatic reminders are useful because many compliance failures start as simple delays. A partner may forget a report deadline, a monitoring action, a bank reconciliation, or a response to a query. If the organisation has a structured reminder cycle, most delays can be fixed before they become formal issues. Escalation then comes into play if the reminder is ignored or if the same issue repeats. The reminder system should be simple, visible, and dated, so the team knows exactly when follow-up started. It should also record who sent the reminder and what the response was. Reminders are not about nagging; they are about creating a reliable chain of accountability so that tasks do not disappear between meetings. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please use the reminder and escalation log so overdue items do not get lost.”
Key principle:
- Timely reminders prevent small delays from becoming control failures.
- Escalation should follow a documented sequence.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why should sub-award data be stored with restricted access?
A: Show answer
Sub-award files often contain bank details, staff information, salary data, donor restrictions, procurement evidence, and sometimes audit findings or allegations. That information should not be broadly visible to everyone in the organisation. Access should therefore be role-based and limited to staff who need the information to perform their duties. Restricting access reduces the chance of unauthorised disclosure, internal misuse, or accidental editing of critical records. The file structure should also protect version integrity so that no one can quietly overwrite the final copy. Secure storage is not only an IT preference; it is a finance control because sensitive records can affect payment decisions, partner trust, and the defensibility of the award file. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please keep sub-award files in restricted folders with role-based access.”
Key principle:
- Sensitive financial data should be need-to-know only.
- File integrity matters as much as confidentiality.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.337 Access to records
- 2 CFR 200.334 Record retention requirements
Q: What should a final review pack for a sub-award include before sign-off?
A: Show answer
The final review pack should confirm that the award file is complete, the final narrative and financial reports have been accepted, bank and cash balances are reconciled, any repayments or unspent funds are accounted for, outstanding findings are resolved or documented, asset treatment is complete, and all approval trails are saved. The pack should also include the final risk assessment, any amendment history, monitoring reports, and the close-out checklist. Senior finance or delegated management should sign off only after the pack shows that the award has reached a clean end state. A strong final review pack prevents the most common close-out problem, which is leaving a file technically open even though staff have already moved on to other work. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please sign off only when the final review pack proves the award is complete.”
Key principle:
- Final sign-off requires a complete evidence pack.
- Open issues must be resolved or clearly documented.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.334 Record retention requirements
- 2 CFR 200.337 Access to records
Rows: 39
